summary: Luu argues that open source projects with large backlogs of ignored pull requests create a systemic barrier to new contributors, even when individual maintainer behavior is reasonable. He describes abandoning contributions to a project after seeing dozens of good patches languishing ignored on the mailing list. The post calls on maintainers with commit access to take ownership of the pull request process or stop soliciting contributions, emphasizing that response time matters more than perfection.
key_points: Many open source projects have massive backlogs of ignored pull requests (116+ in the example)
Individual maintainers may be reasonable, but collectively create a hostile environment for new contributors
New contributors often give up after a few ignored patches with no guidance on where to go next
Fast response time (hours, not weeks) makes a huge difference in encouraging contributions
Maintainers should own the PR process or stop asking for contributions in their repo description
money_quotes: This behavior, eminently reasonable on the part of any individual, results in a systemic failure, a tax on new open source contributors.
It doesn't take egregious individual behavior to create a hostile environment.
I saw a wasteland of good patches that were completely ignored, where the submitter would ping the list a couple times and then give up.
Did it seem worth spending a week to disentangle our IP from the project in order to submit a set of patches that would, in all likelihood, get ignored? No.
If you have commit access to a project that has this problem, please own the process for incoming pull requests (or don't ask for pull requests in your repo description).
key_insight: Open source maintainers must either actively manage their pull request queue or stop soliciting contributions—ignoring contributions creates a systemic barrier to participation that discourages future contributors regardless of how reasonable individual maintainers' constraints are.
